articleTitle: NeuroAgent: LLM Agents for Multimodal Neuroimaging Analysis and Research
explanation:
Picture this: Just as Naruto needed to master multiple chakra natures and coordinate with his shadow clones to tackle complex missions, neuroimaging researchers face the overwhelming challenge of orchestrating multiple brain imaging modalities - structural MRI (sMRI), functional MRI (fMRI), diffusion MRI (dMRI), and PET scans - each requiring its own specialized preprocessing workflow. But unlike Naruto's eventual mastery, most researchers get bogged down in the tedious jutsu of manual pipeline configuration, quality control across heterogeneous toolchains, and the mind-numbing coordination of modality-specific preprocessing steps. This is exactly the chakra-draining problem that NeuroAgent solves through its LLM-driven agentic framework, which automates the complex preprocessing and analysis workflows for multimodal neuroimaging data while supporting interactive downstream analysis through natural language queries.

The technical architecture of NeuroAgent mirrors Naruto's strategic approach to forming the perfect team combination. Just as Naruto learned to coordinate with multiple shadow clones, each with specialized roles, NeuroAgent employs a hierarchical multi-agent architecture powered by a feedback-driven Generate-Execute-Validate engine. The system's agents autonomously generate executable preprocessing code, detect and recover from runtime errors, and validate output integrity - essentially performing the neuroimaging equivalent of Naruto's signature multi-clone coordination jutsu. The framework integrates a sophisticated planning module with intent parsing capabilities (achieving up to 100% accuracy with capable models), workflow construction algorithms, and task identification systems that work together like a perfectly synchronized team attack. The strongest LLM backend, Qwen3.5-27B, demonstrates the system's prowess by reaching 84.8% end-to-end preprocessing step correctness - a performance level that would make even Kakashi proud of such precision and reliability.

When tested on the massive ADNI dataset containing 1,470 subjects (1,000 cognitively normal, 470 with Alzheimer's Disease), NeuroAgent's performance rivals Naruto's ability to handle multiple battlefield scenarios simultaneously. The system successfully processes subjects with complete sMRI and tabular data, while managing subsets with Tau-PET (n=469), fMRI (n=278), and DTI (n=620) - demonstrating the kind of multitasking mastery that would impress even the Fourth Hokage. For Alzheimer's Disease classification using automatically preprocessed multimodal data, the agent ensemble achieves an AUC of 0.9518 with four modalities, significantly outperforming all single-modality baselines. This is like Naruto combining Sage Mode, Nine-Tails chakra, wind release, and shadow clone techniques to create an attack far more powerful than any individual jutsu. The automated recovery system limits manual intervention to edge cases through a Human-In-The-Loop interface, ensuring that researchers only need to step in when truly exceptional situations arise - much like how Naruto eventually learned when to rely on his team versus when to handle challenges independently.

The broader impact of NeuroAgent extends beyond mere automation efficiency - it democratizes access to sophisticated neuroimaging analysis pipelines in the same way that Naruto's journey from academy dropout to Hokage proved that with the right tools and determination, anyone can master complex techniques. By reducing the manual effort required for neuroimaging preprocessing and enabling end-to-end automated analysis pipelines, NeuroAgent removes the traditional barriers between raw brain scan acquisitions and reproducible scientific analysis. The system's ability to handle the multimodal complexity inherent in modern neuroscience research - where meaningful clinical alterations rarely align with a single imaging contrast - represents a fundamental shift toward precision medicine approaches that integrate structural, functional, and molecular brain imaging data. Just as Naruto learned that true strength comes not from mastering a single powerful technique but from understanding how to combine multiple abilities harmoniously, NeuroAgent demonstrates that the future of neuroimaging lies in intelligent systems that can seamlessly coordinate diverse analytical approaches to unlock the full potential of multimodal brain data, ultimately bringing us closer to understanding and treating complex neurological conditions like Alzheimer's disease.
